clean_review: batu caves is an unforgettable blend of culture spirituality and natural beauty. the towering golden statue of lord murugan immediately sets the tone while the colourful staircase leading into the limestone caves creates a striking and almost cinematic first impression. the climb is rewarding not just physically but visually. along the way you get panoramic views of kuala lumpur s outskirts and inside the caves the scale and atmosphere are genuinely awe inspiring. the way sunlight filters through the cave openings gives the space a calm sacred feeling that contrasts beautifully with the lively crowds outside. what makes batu caves special is how accessible yet meaningful it feels. you don t need to be religious to appreciate its importance history and energy. the surrounding area also adds to the experience with local stalls nearby batik shops and a strong sense of malaysian culture. it can be busy and humid but that s part of the authenticity. batu caves doesn t feel like a manufactured attraction it feels alive lived in and deeply rooted in tradition. overall batu caves is absolutely worth visiting. it s one of those places that stays in your memory long after you leave not just for how it looks but for how it feels.

visited this place in malaysia truly it is one of the most iconic and breathtaking place. the massive golden statues of lord murugan at the entrances of the temple is absolutely stunning and good for photography. seeing this statue so close is an unforgettable experience. there were so many colourful stairs i climbed a little bit beacuse batu caves was in my package and after we have to visit another place but the view from that was beautiful. there were plenty of monkeys around. it was hot at thay time so recommend to visit early in the morning. overall batu caves is a must visit when in kuala lumpur.

a must visit if you re in kl the walk isn t that difficult. has all facilities to support large crowds the entry is free and the staff is very helpful. the climb up is a little taxing but the views up top are totally worth it. moreover the cave is beautiful and ancient. there are multiple temples inside.

such an impressive sight on arrival the large statue and brightly multi coloured steps up to the caves. not too busy when we arrived around 09 30 on a weekday morning but a steady stream of people and was getting quite busy as we came back out of the temple. great views as you climb up to the top of the 272 stairs with some monkeys on the way at the top the cave itself is very spacious and impressive with more brightly coloured temples small fee to enter and a smaller one with an opening to the sky which is equally impressive. entry is free apart from the small fees to enter the small temples throughout some at the top and several at the bottom as well. secret cafes and small shops at the bottom to look around too. overall definitely worth a visit but try to get there early morning to beat the crowds.

batu caves was first discovered in a way in the 1930 s but really much before that and opened up as a tourist destination in the 1960s. there are 272 colourful stairs to the car and want to make it to the top you will see you feel great about yourself. the gold standing buddha statue to the right is 140 ft tall and is made by the same person who made the reclining buddha in thailand. definitely a must see place to come to if you are visiting. i highly recommend why for you

we visited batu caves and ramanya caves independently after considering some of the tours offered online which included visits to minor attractions or worse still tat shops. transportation was simple from kl using the mrt and ktm trains overall time was less than 1 hour. outside the caves are vendors selling snacks drinks and gifts. there are also some permanent shops restaurants and toilets. entry to batu cave is free but entry to ramanya cave was 15rm per person. climbing the stairs to batu cave is possible for the average person and once inside there some more stairs but not too many! the shrines and temples are smaller than we expected but still worth the effort. the troops of monkeys are naughty and persistent be careful of your belongings! litter is a problem on the stairs and in the caves there are bins available but they are not monkey proof so the trash is everywhere! looks like lord murugan has got a new coat and looks very smart! ramanya cave is much better decorated with lots of figures depicting scenes very beautiful! there are also quite a few stairs inside to get to the upper areas and some are unequal! there is some renovation work around the entrance so watch your step.

a great spot. i would recommend to go there by yourself. cuz when i did the tour we had about 2 hours there and it wasn t enough for me as i needed and wanted to get some content and hang out with monkeys as well as enjoy the views. it is also very very hot there ofc as everywhere but the steps really make it harder so be prepped . i got myself a fan near the stairs and it made my time much more pleasant

booked a day trip to the one pf the most famous tourist attractions that is batu cabes dedicated to lord vishnu. there s a good number of stairs that need to be climbed to reach the temple as a guide said beware of the gangsters with a tail on your way. overall it s an amazing experience

we were nearby and visited the cave after 5pm to avoid rush hour it was a casual but not effortless climb on the 272 steps rainbow staircase. once you reach the first tier further inside there is another flight of stairs to go to the top most part of the cave and temple. monkeys here are aplenty but still considered well mannered don t touch them they will hiss at you.

the batu caves are an incredibly impressive and culturally enriching destination. the combination of nature spirituality and architecture is truly fascinating. the large main cave with its iconic staircase and temple complex is free to enter and absolutely worth visiting. there is another cave that requires an entrance fee of 30 ringgit which is reasonable. the views cultural significance natural beauty and overall experience deserve 5 stars. the massive limestone formations and the religious setting create a breathtaking and memorable environment. however what i did not appreciate was the cleanliness in certain areas. some parts were quite dirty and there was a very strong smell of dung in several places. in all temple areas visitors are required to remove their shoes. due to the hygienic conditions i unfortunately did not feel comfortable doing so which i found disappointing.

batu caves is more than a tourist attraction it is a place of deep spiritual and cultural significance. the climb up the 272 colourful steps is both challenging and meaningful symbolising faith patience and devotion. at the top the temple cave offers a sense of calm and fulfilment. during thaipusam the entire stairway comes alive with powerful chants rituals and unwavering devotion creating an atmosphere that is intense humbling and unforgettable. visiting batu caves leaves a lasting impression of belief endurance and tradition.a must visit place for every hindus worldwide...

had a great feel when i visit this place with my family. divine as well as scenic nature beautifully constructed temple on the caves makes more attractive. colourful steps to reach the top motivates you to climb up easily. overall it s a fantastic experience for us

my years of li uing to visit this temple was fulfilled . place is filled with devotion. especially the thaipusam days are marvelous. i was able to witness the pray of devotee who was carrying the kavadi for thanking his wishes. well maintained place and renovation is really good so vibrant

a beautiful place and an exceptional experience. be ready to climb a lot of stairs it s tiring but totally worth it. honestly it s a unique experience. the cave from the inside is stunning and the statues the art and the historical atmosphere there make the place truly amazing.

surreal environment unbelievably close to such a big city as kl. the environment is lively full of food and shops. flags flowers and incenses lights up in every corner of this place. lots of hungry monkeys to pay attention to. the entrance is free!

good tourist attractions. we happened to go at the busiest time of the year so we couldn t climb the stairs there were too many worshipers. avoid that time of the year not sure if the religion follows the regular calendar if so we went on 1st february.

such a gorgeous place! the first sighting of the enormous murugan statue with 272 steps leading to the temple is an experience worth having! the climb is mid level difficult and done in about 15 20 minutes depending on your fitness level. the caves with flying bats and the naughty monkeys that are all over makes for a unique sight.

great place to explore. abit crowded when i visited and best to bring umbrella place is too hot. monkeys and pigeons can be found anywhere. stairs going up is to high best not to climb if you have seniors accommpanied. cafes food kiosks and shops are also available.

visiting batu caves was honestly one of the most beautiful and unforgettable spiritual experiences of my life. the moment you reach the entrance the first thing that leaves you speechless is the massive golden statue of murugan standing tall and majestic. the statue looks so powerful and divine that you automatically fold your hands in prayer. seeing it in person feels completely different from photos it s grand glowing and full of positive energy. climbing the colorful steps up to the caves is such a unique experience. with every step the view gets better the breeze feels fresh and the limestone caves look absolutely stunning. inside the natural rock formations temples and peaceful atmosphere make you feel calm and spiritually connected. it s not just a temple visit it feels like nature and devotion coming together beautifully. what touched my heart the most was the warmth of the malaysian tamil people. they are so kind respectful and deeply devoted. the love they have for murugan and their culture is truly inspiring. i honestly felt at home there. their hospitality smiles and strong spiritual vibe made the whole place even more special. i have so much love and respect for the malaysian tamil community batu caves is not just a tourist spot it s an emotion a spiritual journey and a place filled with faith and beauty. if you ever visit malaysia this temple is a must see. you ll come back with peace in your heart and memories for a lifetime. highly recommended for everyone

came to see the batu caves but stayed to watch the monkeys and animals. while the cave formations and sculptures are all very cool found more entertainment in the flocks of pigeons groups of monkeys and the reptile cave. the batu caves are a free attraction but to go down into the cave villa is 10myr. it was a much better usage of time and energy going down as opposed to climbing all the stairs to go to the top. got to see a lot more of the sculptures underground as well as the reptile cave. if you do plan on visiting make sure to wear clothes that cover your knees and shoulders and some good walking shoes as the steps are quite steep and there is a lot of uneven ground.

the scenery and atmosphere is definitely worth checking out with the stairs being a good way to burn off some of that tourist fat and otw down you can buy yourself some sweets to keep the cycle going . on a serious note the cave itself is marvellous and the religious alters of hinduism and their dedication to worshipping are astonishing and inspiring to see. btw beware of the monkeys they be snatching food left and right. a funny sight if you re not the one being targeted .

i had such a great time visiting batu caves! the atmosphere was really lively and there were so many interesting cultural sights to take in. if you re looking for a place rich in culture this is definitely it. the architecture is stunning. i highly recommend checking it out!

a truly unforgettable cultural divine natural landmark batu caves is one of the most fascinating places i ve visited in kuala lumpur. the moment you see the gigantic golden statue of lord murugan and the colourful 272 steps you know you re in for something special it s incredibly photogenic and unique. climbing up the steps might be a bit of a workout in the tropical heat but the views and atmosphere at the top are totally worth it. inside the main cave the cooler calmer temple space offers a really special cultural and spiritual experience that s quite humbling. a few tips if you go arrive early to avoid crowds and heat mornings are the best time for photos and a more peaceful visit. be mindful of the monkeys roaming around they re exciting to watch but keep belongings secure! what i enjoyed most was how batu caves blends natural beauty spiritual energy and vibrant culture all in one place a must visit when you re in kuala lumpur!

the view is truly breathtaking. the inside of the cave is very captivating. be careful with the monkeys they love stealing your belongings. the only issue was the cleanliness and the smell. there was a strong odor of urine and feces probably due to the high number of monkeys. i believe the place would be much more beautiful if more attention were given to cleaning and maintenance. the stairs can be tiring. other than the cleanliness and the smell it was a very beautiful place.

this place is well known and if you re traveling to malaysia you must visit it. enjoy the natural surroundings see the massive buddha statue and climb nearly 300 steps to explore the cave. it s an amazing experience full of fun and adventure.

based upon a recommendation from a friend my wife and headed to the batu caves early in the morning to escape the chaos of tour groups and tourists. it was a wonderful suggestion. while we were not the first people there it was close and our experience was quiet peaceful and pleasant. we had great views of kuala lumpur the freedom to explore the limestone cave temple complex without battling crowds and the macaques which have a reputation for harassing tourists were no where to be seen.

took the commuter train to get here with my girlfriend. great place to appreciate their religion. was doubtful i would be able to climb up the stairs but you can if you pace yourself. i even have a companion with me to keep me from being alone

a wonderful cave turned into a religious temple. a must visit if you are around kuala lumpur and even if you are not religious. very touristic and highly packed with people during weekends. the ramana caves were also nice which talks about the whole ramayana story for that you need to get a ticket. apart from that the whole batu caves is completely free to entry. even the vegetarian restaurant there was pretty good.

a must visit for everyone who travels to kuala lumpur. caves that had been transformed into a beautiful temple and home of cute but naughty little monkeys. but i highly recommend to visit it in the early morning we ve been there at 8 45 am and the moment we were about to leave it was suddenly very crowded. and be careful about the stairs they are very steep and maybe for some people very exhausting. for going down i recommend to use the handrails.

i m not a religious person but this place is visually stunning so much colour and vibrancy. i didn t feel the need to pay the extra and see the other caves and that was the right choice for me. nice climb up the steps into the main cave. transport out there on the train was very easy and straightforward. definitely a must see if in kl

batu caves is one of the most impressive places to visit near kuala lumpur and remarkably the main attraction is completely free to enter because it is still an active hindu temple. the famous rainbow staircase has 272 steps leading up to the main cave and while the climb can be a bit of a workout in the heat the reward at the top is worth it. inside the cave you find an enormous limestone cavern with temples built directly into the rock. the scale of the cave is hard to capture in photos and the atmosphere is very unique. the site itself is fascinating colourful and full of life. monkeys roam around the steps and temple areas which adds to the experience but also means you need to keep an eye on your belongings. one thing visitors should be aware of is that around the train station and entrance area there are people trying to guide tourists toward paid attractions and special caves. the main temple cave is free so if you simply walk toward the giant golden statue and the main staircase you will reach the real attraction. overall batu caves is well worth visiting. it is an extraordinary natural cavern combined with an important religious site and offers one of the most memorable experiences in the kuala lumpur area.

it was our first time visiting a hindu temple and it was truly impressive. i had heard that it was before a major festival and there might be two million people visiting next week. even now it was quite crowded. there were numerous vendors lining both sides of the temple. as we climbed the stairs we spotted several monkeys. all the tour guidebooks had recommended it as a must visit site and we re glad we came!

i have been to batu caves every visit back home and i just love it. to enter you have to pay rm3 for parking cars . there s a total of 272 steps to take you right to the top of the cave. inside you will have the temple. the interesting features is the colorful steps and the view from the top. we went on a saturday evening this time and the view was just amazing.

located at gua lepak batu caves. uncovered hidden gems great starting point for anyone new to via ferrata and zipline. it builds confidence in a safe and fun way before progressing to more difficult and higher routes. a must try for beginners. my love for via ferrata unlocked here. with beautiful scenery expert and pro encouraging friendly guides plus mesmerizing drone pics and vids you won t regret it with fz climb via ferrata batu caves!

sacred hindu temples inside the caves makes you feel devine in malaysia. there are couple of temples in the base also. please mind that there is a dress code to enter the temple via stairs. also there are so many veg restaurants and souvenir shops. it s mostly crowded

well it s a temple so definitely 5 star ! watch out malaysia series on our youtube channel name is kp travel tales . only issue was lord murgan statue which was under restoration when we visited so that one thing left to see. there are more then 260 steps that you need to climb in order to enter the main cave ! but it was great !! definitely a place to visit when you in malaysia

batu caves is a really impressive place and definitely worth a visit if you re in kuala lumpur. the area is well kept and the huge statue and colorful stairs make it very unique. there are lots of monkeys around which makes the visit even more interesting just watch your belongings! . you ll also find many food and drink vendors nearby so it s easy to grab a snack. entry to the main temple cave is free which is great. there s also another cave you can visit for 10 ringgit if you want to explore more. overall a beautiful and lively place with a great atmosphere.

visited batu caves in malaysia and it was a truly memorable experience. the climb up the colorful steps was very enjoyable and energizing with a vibrant atmosphere all around. once at the top the sight from the hill was absolutely beautiful offering great views and a refreshing sense of calm. the caves themselves are mesmerizing with their massive limestone formations creating a majestic and spiritual ambiance. the blend of natural beauty cultural significance and scenic views makes batu caves a must visit attraction. it s a wonderful place for sightseeing photography and experiencing malaysia s heritage all in one visit.

a profound and must visit site especially for hindus. the historical cave temple is a powerful spiritual experience. admission is free but plan your transport wisely. a grab car to get here is affordable but returning can cost double due to limited availability. my strong advice arrive early by train for the easiest access or book your return grab ride well in advance to lock in a better rate. i hope malaysian tourism board should spend some money to maintain this place.

one of the top tourist places in kl. you can reach this place in kommuter train from kl sentral. train frequency may vary based on the day that you travel. you can also grab for 40 mins from city center. check the temple open times before you plan. during the month of january it may be crowded due to thai poosam festival. it has steep steps so be prepared to burn your calories. once you reach the top of the hill it will be a divine place. highly recommended. plenty of parking available. few meters away from train station. entrance is free. lot of monkeys are there so keep your items safe.

it was about 40 min train ride from kl sentral and the place is just next to the batu cave station. it s a beautiful sight and a key tourist attraction in kl. it was super hot during the day so you need have sufficient amount of water to get hydrated.

the dark caves hasn t opened post pandemic so it is just now a visit to lord murugan temple. it s very crowded and lots of people persuading visitors to buy this or that. the charm of visiting this place was a visit to the unesco world heritage site of dark cave. which isn t happening anymore.

great place to visit for its vibrancy colour and people. we didn t do the 270 or so stairs to the top but there was enough to see on the ground. only 3 minutes walk from the exit of railway station. note that no shoes are to be worn in the temples. enjoy.

batu caves is a famous limestone hill and hindu temple complex located just north of kuala lumpur malaysia. it is best known for its massive golden statue of lord murugan and the 272 colorful steps leading to the main cave temple. the site is an important religious destination especially during the thaipusam festival and is also a popular tourist attraction for its natural caves shrines and panoramic views.

when thinking of malaysia and kuala lumpur the image of the batu caves immediately comes to mind. what a joy it was to actually visit this religious hindu temple. even if you are a foreigner you can still visit this place for free easily. just make sure that knees and shoulders are covered. if not you need to buy or rent a scarf or similar. there are many monkeys be aware. just don t get close to them don t feed them or don t smile to them. or they might bite. definitely try to come before 8am it is so worth it. it is not crowded and not hot. easy to come here by grab return it costed me less than 40 rmb. when visiting kuala lumpur don t miss this place!

largest vishnu murti with other temples of hindu deity shiva ganesha hanuman . climb approx 245 stairs to enter the cave and immerse the beauty of the culture. there are many places to shop although expensive and food outlets. in malaysia this is must place to go and gain the beauty!

batu caves were an experience in what can go wrong when a natural wonder is used for commercial purposes. walking up to the site we saw lots of rubbish about which was disappointing. on arrival we witnessed what is like a dishevelled 1970 s theme park with the largest number of tourists we had seen in kl in one place. the super sized sculptures were great and the cheeky monkeys pinching food and scooting about were a laugh . walking up the 270 odd steps to the main caves was great exercise and looking up at the limestone formations once at the top was wonderful. although far from a spiritual experience it was another one of those quirky kl attractions to experience and decide for yourselves. in saying all of this i am glad we went .

batu caves is a very beautiful and colorful place. the temple area and the giant statue are really impressive. even though there were a lot of people when we visited mostly indian families and pilgrims probably because of a religious period we still managed to take some very beautiful photos. i also got a lovely henna design on my hand for a good price which was a nice experience. that said some areas were quite dirty and there were unpleasant smells in certain spots. i also felt uncomfortable at times because of some persistent stares from a few men even though i was dressed respectfully for the place. some areas require you to remove your shoes. personally i chose not to enter those sections because the ground did not seem clean enough to walk barefoot. regarding touts we didn t have any problems because we were aware of it beforehand and simply ignored the people calling out to us. we didn t have any problems with the monkeys because we didn t feed them and kept our distance just be careful and hold your belongings properly. one tip if you visit during a busy period don t order a grab directly from the batu caves pickup point. we did and it took almost an hour just to get out of the entrance traffic. it s better to walk a bit away from the caves before booking a ride. overall it s a very nice place to see just be prepared for crowds.

batu caves is a great place to visit if you re in kuala lumpur. the colorful stairs are eye catching and the huge golden statue at the entrance is really impressive in person. the climb can be a bit tiring especially during the day but it s totally worth it once you reach the top. the cave and temple area feel unique and full of character. just keep an eye on your belongings because the monkeys around the stairs can be quite cheeky. it s best to come early in the morning to avoid the heat and crowds. overall a memorable spot and definitely worth a visit.

very nice place and very significant to hindu belief. i did toom afew steps on the stairs but came back. to weather and time i was there was hot and close to noon time. lots og monkey around so watch out for these pick pockets. dont feed them.

batu caves is an important religious and cultural site and i respect its significance to the local community. however as a visitor i felt that some of the recent construction and lighting inside the caves does not blend well with the natural limestone environment. the bright led lighting and concrete structures take away from the natural beauty and atmosphere of the cave. with more thoughtful design and softer lighting this place could better preserve its natural charm while still serving its religious purpose.

was a pretty average experience and very underwelming especially inside the caves. the whole place smells quite a bit and there is a lot of trash everywhere. all the steps up didnt seem to bad. very touristy but we didnt have to much problem with to many people as we went real early. lots of better things to do in kl

the site is beautiful and the colorful stairs are an iconic kuala lumpur landmark. we visited during a religious festival arriving at 9 am meant avoiding the worst crowds but it got very packed by the time we left. the main square was swarming with pigeons and covered in droppings making the idea of entering the temple barefoot as some were doing quite disgusting. while the view from the bottom is stunning the actual cave at the top is underwhelming. i felt quite nauseous inside due to the smell devotees were carrying milk on their heads as offerings and the scent of sour milk was everywhere. honestly the climb in the sweltering heat isn t worth it for what s at the top.

if you love nature adventure history and animals batu caves is definitely worth visiting. we traveled there using the ktm komuter seremban line which was cheap fast clean and comfortable. once we exited the ktm station and headed toward batu caves we were approached by promoters from a nearby cave attraction. many visitors were guided in that direction and we initially thought it was the main entrance to batu caves. there was a small fee of rm30 per person which seemed reasonable at the time so we entered. however we later realized that this attraction is not the actual batu caves and it was not worth it for us. tip for first time visitors ignore the promoters and walk straight toward the main batu caves entrance even if you see construction along the way. you ll know you re in the right place when you see a large crowd pigeons and monkeys near the iconic entrance. the views and scenery at batu caves are beautiful and impressive. however the smell from the animals especially monkeys and birds can be unpleasant at times. wearing a face mask can help if you re sensitive to odors. lastly come prepared wear sunscreen bring an umbrella or water bottle. do not wear shorts. long pants or long skirts are required to enter the temple area or you may have to pay extra for cover ups especially for women. overall it was a great experience and we hope these tips help you enjoy your visit even more. hope you have a wonderful time at batu caves

it is worth making the trip to batu caves but it s about 30 minute visit. the first thing to be advised of is when you approach the caves there are a couple of official looking individuals waving you to go left which is actually a different cave that charges an entrance fee ramayana cave . you reach batu caves if you walk straight a couple of minutes. please be aware of the modest dress code to enter the caves and the steps aren t bad if you re moderately in shape.

the trash is overwhelming. the smell of poop is terrible. this temple is honestly the most disgusting place i ve ever been. we went early around 7am to miss the crowds. save yourself the time don t go. outside around the temple smells worse. actually gag i think it was genuine human poop somewhere. if you walk up those steps barefoot i ll say a prayer for your feet. trust me the trash is everywhere. when you get to the top guess what as gross as the bottom. don t go if you re sensitive to smells.

batu caves formed approximately 400 million years ago are one of the holiest hindu sites outside of india. they are famous for their 272 colorful steps we climbed them and the enormous 43 meter high golden statue of the god murugan. the whole thing is impressive you feel so small compared to the vastness of everything around you. it s definitely worth a visit and it s worth dedicating a day to exploring this beautiful place.

a must visit while visiting here in malaysia. entrance is free for the most part except when going inside some of the caves. of course the statue and steps are the highlight of this attraction. be mindful that it does get super busy it s very humid and hot especially in the summer steps can be slippery especially inside the cave monkeys birds are everywhere. make sure you dress appropriately covered up and breathable .
have to visit once in life time for thai poosam. great vibe. pure devotional cultural mix. we visit in the early morning so not much crowded. local train is a best option to travel.

this is a very famous site and it was quite a hike up the stairs into the cave but i was underwhelmed by the whole visit. i m not saying you shouldn t visit but i was expecting more as i have visited many similar places and they really should do a better job at maintaining the site.

currently the tall statue is under renovation batu caves is a very famous hindu temple and tourist spot in kuala lumpur malaysia. simple explanation it is a limestone hill with a huge cave temple inside. the entrance has a big golden statue of lord murugan one of the tallest in the world . you must climb 272 colourful steps to reach the main cave. it s an important religious place for hindus and also a popular sightseeing location. people visit for prayers photography and the amazing cave view.

easy to get to but crowded with people and monkeys. be careful with your bags and mobile phones as these monkeys are very quick and skilled at snatching your belongings. there are also many pigeons wear a face mask to protect against the smell and germs from pigeon droppings. the cave is cool and comfortable inside perfect for exploring.

great place. welll worth a visit despite or perhaps because of all the steps. knee length shorts seem to be ok for men despite notices to contrary. caves are free although visitors are herded ushered to ramayana cave if you arrive by train. see separate entry for ramayana cave.

when arrived they will direct you round to the left to pay rm15 entrance fee to go inside some cave. but the actual highlight was the colourful stairs leading up to the cave on top. this is free! just turn right as you came in from the drop off point and keep going to the front section of the temple until you find the giant statue and the colourful stairs

its a big cave if youve ever been to a cave its one of those just bigger one. women need to cover the legs so if you dont have pants it costs 15 for a scarf you get to keep it. otherwise the caves are free but the temples are not. there is a lot of stairs and extreme heat. the most fun part about this is the monkeys this place is a bit overhyped if you ask me but its free though so all you have to pay is the trip there. all that said unless youre into hindu temples i would say its not worth the time.

go early in the morning between 7am to 9am during the weekdays as weekends are always super busy with a lot of tourists.1. you beat the crowds and the tour buses 2. if you are visiting early you avoid being in the intense heat so the time of year is also important. try to visit march through july jan feb is very busy with massive crowds for the thaipusam festival 3.dress code for women must cover their knees and shoulders no open cleavage etc and if you forget you can buy or rent sarongs at the entrance.4. it s easy to get to by grab taxi or you can take the ktm train that drops you off directly at bath caves. now for the pov what a wonderful experience nothing i would have thought of it being. it looks simple at first but then you continue to walk through you realize you are somewhere different as you embrace all the different colors of the rainbow you see monkeys everywhere that aided to the experience. the area is enriched with spiritual culture. i believe i saw 4 smaller temples walking around but the temple you see walking up 272 stairs is the cherry on top. once you collect your breath it s taken away again at the beautiful structure architecture. it s hard to believe it was once done years and years ago by hand but done beautifully to withstand the test of time. it was a privilege to be able to experience this beautiful place i have a few videos and pics to share on google but if you want to see a full video you can visit my travel youtube channel adventuresintolalaland youtube i hope this helps someone as they venture to see batu caves as well

we visited this place when the large statue of lord murugan was under maintenance. the steps up to the caves are colourful though we didn t make the climb. one thing to note is that there is a dress code for entrance such as no shorts or revealing clothes. there were lots of monkeys and birds around the place. there are colourful temples shops cafes and toilets conveniently located here.
highly recommend. the place is gorgeous. its a wonder. i enjoyed my time there sightseeing and all. highly recommend if you are in kuala lumpur dont skip batu caves.

if you are visiting kuala lumpur batu caves is an absolute must see. this holy hindu shrine is not just a place of worship but a breathtaking fusion of nature art and spirituality. my recent visit left me deeply moved by the atmosphere and the sheer scale of the limestone formations. the grand entrance and iconic idols as soon as you arrive you are greeted by the massive lord kartikeya murugan idol. even though it was partially covered for renovation during my visit its presence is still awe inspiring. nearby the hanuman idol stands incredibly tall and majestic a perfect representation of strength and devotion. the craftsmanship across all the statues is remarkable showcasing the intricate beauty of tamil architecture. one of the best things about batu caves is that it is inclusive people of all faiths are welcome to explore and experience the sanctity of the site. additionally there is no entry ticket for the main cave making it accessible to everyone. the temple complex the site is home to multiple temples dedicated to various deities including lord ganesh lord shiva goddess parvati lord kartikeya maa durga lord vishnu and hanuman. each shrine has its own unique energy. i was particularly struck by the lord vishnu idol it is exquisitely beautiful and radiates a sense of peace that stays with you. the journey into the cave the climb up the rainbow stairs is iconic but the real reward is at the top. the temples built inside the cave are unlike anything i ve seen before. the main cave is vast and the way it opens up at the top of the mountain allows natural sunlight to stream in creating a divine ethereal glow over the inner shrines. it feels as though the heavens are looking down into the temple. nature and amenities beyond the spiritual aspect the natural beauty of the site is stunning. i found a beautiful stream that flows through the area ending in a serene waterfall a perfect spot to take a moment of reflection away from the crowds. for those worried about logistics parking there is ample parking space available which makes the visit stress free if you are driving or taking a private car. environment while it can get busy the area is well maintained and provides a great mix of culture and nature. final verdict batu caves is a masterpiece of south indian heritage in the heart of malaysia. whether you are there to offer prayers to lord shiva and maa durga or simply to admire the limestone caves and the waterfall you will leave with a sense of wonder. despite the ongoing renovations to the kartikeya statue the site remains a 5 star experience. pro tip wear comfortable shoes for the stairs and remember to dress modestly out of respect for the temples!

very cool hindu temple located in a cave in a cliffside on the outskirts of the city. it has a massive statue out front which is the largest in malaysia. there are monkeys along the steps and inside the cave which are pretty chill.
a landmark pace in kualalumpur rich in history and tradition... if you climb the steps on top of the hill there is a huge cave and has couple temples inside... really amazing...

lovely spiritual place and the main attraction of kuala lumpur. it has been refurbished in advance of the 2026 celebrations starting on 01 01 26. when we srrived we were ushered toward the caves on the left where we paid rm15.00 to get in and see ram s story. after that we proceeded to the main area where the colourful steps and golden statue. the scaffolding was up so we only got to see the top half.

super amazed with the batu caves. the history about it is amazing. i can just sit and look up. there were some tourists but mot overwhelmingly a lot. there monkeys that can take your food away. but nothing scary.

batu caves delivered an unforgettable saturday morning adventure for our family. the murugan statue stole the show with its jaw dropping beauty leaving us mesmerized right from the entrance. the vibrant energy hit immediately withbusy but manageable crowds that created a lively buzz without chaos. climbing the 272 colorful steps offered stunning views and playful monkeys pro tip no food as they re aggressive thieves our son cheered on stray dogs that scared them off haha! . inside the main cave a serene yet electric calm reigned roosters crowed bats and pigeons fluttered amid hindu shrines blending meditation with joyful craziness and we lingered 30 minutes soaking it in. descending felt festive with live singers sparking impromptu dances followed by mangosteen durian and ice cream at nearby stalls. we wrapped up at ramayana cave where helpful guides explained rama s story and symbols. absolutely worth it and recommended our experience was pure magic that words can t capture!

batu caves a must see in kuala lumpur! just a short trip from the city center this iconic hindu temple complex is an experience for the senses! climb the 272 colorful steps to be rewarded with the stunning sight of the cathedral cave and its intricate shrines. a few tips for your visit beware of the monkeys! they are bold and will snatch anything shiny or edible. keep your belongings secure. dress modestly. shoulders and knees should be covered sarongs are available to rent if needed . go early! beat the crowds and the heat for a more peaceful visit. stay hydrated. the climb is steep and the malaysian sun is no joke! the blend of spiritual devotion natural beauty and vibrant culture here is truly unforgettable. have you been? what was your favorite part? batucaves kualalumpur malaysiatravel travelasia wanderlust temple lordmurugan adventureawaits cavetemple traveldeeper unesco ipulledamuscleclimbingthesestairs location batu caves selangor malaysia

a beautiful place with sacred temples and impressive scenery. the atmosphere is spiritual and unique. overall a great place to visit although there is quite a lot of garbage along the way which slightly takes away from the experience.

we visited the dark cave missed the main one for some reason. i s pretty nice display of hindu regilion and cooler inside. guess we have to come back for another visit for the real climbing challenge. don t be like us and head to the right side for main cave the left side is dark cave that need an entrance ticket to enter.

as a top 10 reviewer in the world here is most disgusting attraction i ever been to rubbish everywhere rats monkeys very dangerous stairs inside cave. the smell will make you chuck up definitely not recommended here

clean spacious and colorful with newly built souvenir shops and spacious parking lot inside...surprisingly the parking fee for car is very cheap just rm3 per entry. and theres no entrance fee for individual. but u need to wear proper clothing otherwise need to wear covered skirt for a fee.. recommended to bring small fan drinks and hat. its streinuous trek up the stair but then theres cute monkeys to look at and u will be greeted with cool breeze and big cave when reaching the top. definitely one of the recommended activity to do

a very beautiful and airy place on the outskirts of kuala lumpur city. the base of these caves is a famous hindu temple with a historical significance. there re coffee shops and food joints for visitors to savour before and after visiting the place. there re two temples on both sides of the compound and more than 100 colourful stairs to the cave entrance. the caves are very old but maintained nicely. there re small temples in the caves as well. ample parking space for the place and a very relaxing yet enjoyable scenery from the place. the brass golden statue of the lord is the biggest attraction point of this spot.

despite the frequent climbs this place is a natural wonder that isn t overly exhausting. it was so enjoyable to hike or climb there that i never wanted to leave. once you reach the top a breathtaking view awaits you. i highly recommend anyone passing through to visit this forest and go for a walk.
parking is free but limited. the entrance fee is rm10 for malaysian and rm30 for foreigners.

well if this place happens to be on your hiking or walking trail then yes do visit it. the idea of driving to this place especially after four hours in traffic is baffling. it is perhaps 100 meters of crowded boardwalk and while the moss is there it is not overwhelming. i saw comparable moss on my hiking trails 10 6 12 .

lovely place! no need for a sunny weather being inside the clouds makes it even more magical no mosquito but a bit chilly so take a jacket or sweater. entrance fee is 30rm person trail is very easy around 30min. you need a 4wd to go up there and down 30 45min each way so i recommend to book your transport in advance private driver or book a tour because you probably won t find a grab to take you and walking there is not really possible road with no sidewalk on several km .

a mossy forest also known as a cloud forest is a type of high altitude tropical and subtropical forest characterized by a consistent heavy layer of cloud cover mist and high humidity. this unique environment creates ideal conditions for the growth of a dense carpet of moss lichens ferns and other epiphytes plants that grow on other plants that cover the trunks branches and ground of the forest. mossy forests are a rare and fragile ecosystem found in tropical and subtropical mountainous regions around the world. significant examples are located in southeast asia this is one of the most well known regions for mossy forests particularly in malaysia cameron highlands indonesia the philippines and sri lanka.

compare to sunny weather and raining foggy weather i prefer the rainy one. you re literally in the cloud everything looks so magical. i m so obsessed with the view. even though you can t see anything far away the view surrounds you and it s already made you amazed. i hope heaven looks like this. if you think it s about to rain you better bring your own raincoat but if you don t you need to pay rm5 for their disposable raincoat.

had been wanting to come here ever since i saw a poster many years back. except i had wanted to hike here i was quite surprised to find out that the trail is a bunch of wooden steps. had fun walking about and clicking pics at every corner. the ticket price is rather high though 30rm per person for foreigners regardless of whether you have an ikad or not.

a must see in cameron highland. we skipped the half day tour package as we only wanted to go see the forest. just go to nova highlands hotel at 0830 or 1030 to catch the transportation to go up to the main entrance of mossy forest. fee is rm35 rm10 for entry fee per head. jeep will go when it fulfilled a minimum 4 passengers. the entire boardwalk will cover around 45 55 minutes and you will experience a cooler weather and mossy forest. great for family too. avoid weekends as this place will be very crowded. overall we enjoyed our time up there.

trail lasted about 15 to 30 minutes its a forest so there is lots of nature around and especially lots of moss trees when we reached the viewing tower view was breathtaking i ve attached a picture of the view i thoroughly enjoyed the nature walk it was a pleasant experience and is highly recommended. please note we went when it was not raining and early morning so if you go when it s raining the steps may be slippery so be careful also i wore shorts i was cold initially but when start walking it s okay also you need to buy tickets online

mossy forest is a nice stop along the highlands route. the walk takes about 30 minutes along a wooden boardwalk. it was a bit cold early in the morning and there were quite a few people but the atmosphere is interesting with lots of moss and greenery. worth a quick visit if you re in the area.

parking is free but limited. the entrance fee is rm10 for malaysian and rm30 for foreigners. the trail is an easy walk with a wooden staircase and walkway and the view from the top is beautiful. we went without a guide so we didn t get to learn about the history of forest and plants. be prepared for the cold temperatures and note that there are no toilets available.

we visited here on our last day in cameron highlands. after checking out early we drove to this point as a detour on our way back home. the drive up here was challenging as the narrow roads often meant we had to stop to allow the cars from the opposite lane to squeeze past. we got our tickets from the office near the beginning of the trail where we had to register ourselves before entering the forest. the walkway has plenty of sinages and informative material on the mossy forest so it was nice to pause and learn about the natural biodiversity of this place. there is also a lookout tower where you can climb and soak in the gorgeous vista of the mountainside with an unobstructed 360 degree view because the platform at the top is above the canopy. i think this is my favourite spot. as the clouds rolled by the forest are covered with mist and you can feel the dampness and the droplets of water. the entire hike takes about an hour and it is a fun and easy one as the path is clearly laid out. it was a great activity to mark the end of our long weekend at cameron highlands.

great experience but the walkway is damaged in certain parts and the steps aren t of equal length. also the jeep ride up here is an adventure on it s own with the front door swinging open on its own...

the misty forest area is currently closed temporarily starting 25 october 2025 until further notice. the forestry department has put up signs and barriers due to safety concerns and ongoing restrictions in the cameron highlands forest reserve. better check for updates before coming still a beautiful place just not accessible right now.

it is necessary to purchase entry tickets online as on site ticket counters are unavailable. the weather is pleasantly cool. the boardwalk entry fee is rm10 per person and does not include access to the hiking trail. driving here is quite challenging due to the narrow roads but the experience is worthwhile for nature enthusiasts.

stunning view on the top and the trail n.1 which brings you here it s gonna leave you breathless. just pay attention to the tickets if you reach the top of the mossy forest with a rented scooter few guys in the parking lot will take pictures of your plate and instantly send that to the policeman nearby. just leave the scooter at the bottom of the main road or at the beginning of the trail.

nice place to visit when you are visiting the cameron highlands. it is of course very popular among tourists and therefore you will be visiting the place with a lot of other tourists. still if you have sufficient time it is worth a visit.

this is a cloud forest. the trees are mostly covered in moss. many other tourists here so can get rimmed from tours bring people. to see it properly i d come beginning or the end of the day not on a tour. we were on a tour and there was no cloud...hence my suggestions. it is still very pretty and scenic though.

the mossy forest in cameron highlands is a magical misty escape. with mosscovered trees cool air and unique flora like pitcher plants it is a dream for nature lovers and photographers. the boardwalk trail is easy to follow and offers stunning views of the lush otherworldly landscape. however it can get a bit crowded at times especially when tour groups arrive so going early is a good idea if you want a quieter experience. despite that it s still well worth a visit. just remember to respect the environment this place is delicate and deserves to be preserved. a must visit if you re in cameron highlands and love the outdoors.
loved the magical vibe of mossy forest such a unique experience in cameron highlands. but the rm30 fee for foreigners feels a bit too high for the short walk.
rm10 per person or usually hv to take the package rm50 person 4x4 with entrance ticket. just a short 20 30min walk max...

it is a beautiful misty and mossy forest. there are information panels describing the plants and their characteristics in both english and malay. personally i think the trip will be more interesting with a guided tour while on a road trip. we went during ramhdan days at early morning. not many people. we enjoy the nature flowers and birds. the trails are easy if only because the stairs can be difficult for older people. but as long as one does not have a knee problem it shouldn t be a big deal. i ve seen many eldery but they manage to walk all parts of the tracks. entry ticket is rm10 you need a tour agency to bring you to the entrance by van which costs rm25. if you are driving your own car your car will be blocked by a guard at the intersection before you reach the entrance. i would advise against driving yourself even without the regulations because the road is horrible. overall a great trip which can be combined with a visit to boh tea plantations.
a wonderful place worth visiting. entrance ticket is 30 ringgit. any car can go up the road well but your heart must be strong

so beautiful! you have to go on a tour you re not allowed to explore on your own. but the tour showed us all kinds of plants including orchids carnivorous plants and different medicinal plants. he also showed us lots of gorgeous view points

unless you get a guide to do a trek inside the forest where he will properly show and explain things then it isn t actually worth it considering also the price we paid! as we were pretty unprepared and with only one day at the cameron highlands we just got a 4x4 driver rm35 p in front of nova hotel where they usually gather who basically just drove us to the spot collected the entrance fee in cash rm30 p for foreigners and then he let us walk on the wooden boardwalk on our own a 20 min return walk . as mossy forest is the highlight of the area and considering that all the farms attractions strawberry bee cactus etc were of no interest to us but rather to families with kids we could just have skipped cameron highlands entirely!

the journey up was quite trying especially as i was suffering from a rather severe bout of food poisoning. but it was worth it and a definite must visit if you re in the area. the boardwalk makes it way easier to move around compared to when i came as a child. was not too crowded when i was there perhaps because the malaysian sch holidays has ended it was also a weekday.

mossy forest is a natural site that is very pleasant to visit if you plan to hike in the jungle or have a simple walk after reaching the top spot by car. to better explain you can reach the mossy forest via car straight to the top spot marked by mossy forest boardwalk in the map below alternatively you can follow the same road by walking starting point bee factory arrival mossy forest which takes 2 30 hours. this route provides some wonderful sights to enjoy. once on top you can pay 30 ringgit to walk along a wooden brigde 600 meters into the jungle not really worth it mossy forest boardwalk in the map below if you wish to experience a full hiking experience you can reach the mossy forest via jungle another path from the forementioned identified with number 10 in the map below . this is a difficult track and requires some kind of preparation keep in mind you may find some signs warning that the path is blocked i wouldn t advise taking a route you are not confident enough you can handle you need good stamina hiking boots insect spay and off track experience . i also wouldn t recommet said track if it has recently rained. if you follow this path i advise you go back via the other road the one mentioned in the previous section that can be covered also by car completing the entire ring.

we visited mossy forest in early march with our kids aged 5 and 11. we only made it halfway through the elevated boardwalk before turning back as the kids were tired. our tour guide brought us in a 4x4 as the roads to the forest are quite rugged and narrow. the views inside were amazing and the weather was perfect. the forest blanketed in moss and ferns looked magical and we even saw a few pitcher plants.

i got lucky that the normal route is under maintenance so they opened a alternative route during the maintenance. the route goes into the forest which tourists have to climb up steep hill step over logs and rocks. was an exciting tour for people like me who had never went on hiking before.

this is a great way to explore the cloud forest with some great views. with pitcher plants and other flora growing naturally you get to experience what a natural cloud forest is like without needing to hack through the forest with a knife. the bird life was loud.

it s a great experience to be here. a must visit place if you are going to cameron highlands. you can reach this by your own car or hire a land rover tour guide. the entrance ticket to the mossy trail is rm 10 for malaysians. different rates for foreign tourists. recommended place to visit.

beautiful place truly. one of a kind experience but here are some things to consider 1. inaccessible by grab so you ll have to get a tour operator who will overcharge you 2. the entry to the forest itself is 30rm per head which again is a little steep considering it s less than a km hike. 3. if you re going to walk there i suggest mentally preparing yourself for a steep incline that cars can t manage good experience overall

way up there is not that bad just a little narrow and steep. a normal car is able to be driven up but parking space is limited. we went during the weekday so no traffic nor parking issue. tickets for locals are rm10 for adults rm5 for kids for international visitors rm30. bridges are built so that visitors will not be able to cross over to destroy the moss. very beautiful mossy forest inside the clouds. if you re lucky you ll be able to spot some critters.

totally recommend. dependent on the weather of course but the beautiful dense forrest was so interesting. locals will say you need a tour or a 4x4 to get up but you can easily get up with a scooter. either way if you want an exceptional experience and some guidence on you way use discover cameron travel and tour. they were amazing and reasonable price. 30rm entry

best place in cameron highlands! we went there in the morning the route was steep and tricky some parts were indeed scary luckily we re driving an suv so it s ok. however the view along the route was super way better tea plantation view compared to the normal tourist spot! the forest itself was great there were some kind of gated steps for the trail which made it very easy for most people including my kids to walk and explore the forest. they ve got a tower in the middle of the trail and the view from up there is just superb

exploring the mossy forest was an unforgettable adventure! the lush moss created a mesmerizing cinematic effect enveloping the scenery in a dream like atmosphere. the tranquility and humidity made it feel like walking through a cloud heightening the sense of immersion in nature. listening attentively to the sounds and identifying different plant species added an exciting dimension to the experience. the journey along the scenic road offered beautiful viewpoints of the tea farms enhancing the overall enjoyment. it was truly a remarkable excursion that left a lasting impression on us!

we read many confusing reports about getting up to the mossy forest but i hope the below will clear a few things up here is the deal with getting up to the mossy forest as of december 2024 they are repairing the boardwalk so you go on an alternate route through another section of the forest which is very muddy and uneven so bring waterproof shoes. you cannot drive yourself all the way up to the forest due to a roadblock that only lets guides through but you can park at the roadblock and walk the over 1 km up a very steep road the rest of the way. i wouldn t recommend this. we were told this wasn t allowed but there were people doing this. what you should do is hire a driver guide through your hotel or you can get one on the way in the nova highlands hotel carpark which is what we did. it was about 100 ringits total for the two of us which included the necessary permit. possibly on the high side but we were on our own and he was great. we would have missed a lot of the plants and interesting information about the forest had we been on our own. the tour took about an hour and a half all up and we stopped for a look at the tea plantation on the way down. well worth it but a bit disappointed the boardwalk wasn t open
it was a mesmerising experience everyone must visit this place once in a lifetime
nice boardwalk but can t go into the rest of the forest without a tour guide booked beforehand. 30 ringgits per person cash only or qr code for tourist coming by yourself make sure to bring cash

mossy forest is a captivating destination where visitors can experience the charm of a lush moss covered forest. even those who don t exercise regularly will find the trails easy and enjoyable. it s an ideal spot for sightseeing and immersing yourself in nature.

yesterday without explanation of our guide we weren t guided to the area advertised in the tour but from what i had seen in the first few meters into the park it looked nice and mossy. we paid 10 rm extra besides our tour costs and are foreigners. but it was loud as all tours talked loudly kids whined and cried and guides did a lot of explaining. i didn t like it because it ruined the atmosphere if you cannot listen to the jungle. pro tip if you are good on foot and do not mind a bit of an adventure you are better off here if you also want to listen to the jungle and not just see it today we did trail 10 12 starting in tanah rata and ended at the power maintenance station close to cameron highland resort on our own and saw a great mossy forest and enjoyed it completely for ourselves including listening to siamang monkey voices from the distance. i place a video of the sounds and remark it as trail 12 10. you will see the difference.

a most beautiful and mysterious forest. the 4x4 jeep we ve hired was the best decision we ve made as we weren t sure whether to drive on our own or not. our local driver guide was an amazingly knowledgeable guy who could point out all the exotic plants in the forest and more info on everything else. as of feb 2024 the mossy forest entry rates for a local is rm 10 person and rm 30 adult foreigner. our driver had also stopped the vehicle to admonish a group of visitors who d stopped by the side of the trail picking off the delicate mosses off the side of the mountain. he explained how the mosses will not survive in any other weather outside of the area plus the immeasurable damage done to the mosses picked. the board walk trek takes about a quick 1 hour to complete but might take more if you re a photo enthusiastic person like myself . the forest is amazing mysteriously half shrouded in clouds earning it s name garden in the clouds. here s the not so nice bit most first timers won t know about driving to mossy forest unless you re driving a pick up truck a 4wd or 4x4 do not use your own vehicle to climb up this treacherous road. a normal car that experiences any problems along the way will have to pay a minimal fee of rm 1k for towing only not to mention inconveniencing the rest of the visitors on the trail line that could cause a jam that can last hours. one normal van reportedly had 2 tires loosing air only to find sharp stones embedded into them. we personally saw a sedan went through multiple potholes nightmare for suspensions and then skidding on certain parts going up while holding back all the other 4x4s. some of the pot holes and there are alarmingly many! are a few inches deep and about 1 to 5 feet wide. there are also cracks and splits on the path about 3 4 of the way up with most stretches tilting to the side. the path is also very small making navigation hard if drivers are not confident in handling a two way traffic on such narrow roads. some corners and narrow turnings have sharp drops. overall if you can hire a 4x4 driver to bring you around. less stress of damaging your own car and being able to enjoy the whole ride in the local country way. important note try not to attempt to hike up on your own if possible. the path is dangerous with the vehicles going up and down plus dangerous drops and slippery roads. some times it can get so misty and cloudy you won t be able to see beyond a few feet of you. the locals shared stories of rescue missions which made them weary of hikers. they will not hesitate to admonish you for hiking just to save money out of their experiences. hiring the 4x4s are around rm 55 60 per person.

nice mossy forest which able to reached within few minutes using the boardwalk which located around 2000m above sea level. entrance fee is 30rm for adult international walk up the boardwalk and reach the first tower to have a good landscape view. however to proceed the trek to the peak you need a permit. good for a short family trek with young kids but the self drive towards the entrance is very challenging with heaps of narrow bends fortunately not much vehicle when we went otherwise you need super driving skills especially if driving non 4 wd drive .

no ticket counter service has to buy online. no checking ticket also you can walk in directly. road are narrow up to mossy forest entrance can come by own vehicle. beware inside mossy forest some pathways are broken rusty nails are exposed and handrails are shaking.
very touristic obviously but just a short walk in the forrest. it s okay because of the viewing point at 2km altitude but the rest is a very standard forest. 30rm per person is quiet expensive

apparently mossy forest is close to public until july 2024. they are doing a renovation to improve tourist safety in the future. for the time being only registered hikers are allowed to use the trail in order to reach the entrance to mt. irau. the road to reach this place is actually improved compared to the past. but for safety purposes the forestry department only allowed 4wd truck jeep tourist agency to reach this point. spectacular view even from the entrance. if it is open again to the public i really recommend this place to visit.
the views are great but the price seems to be a bit too high for what you get. it s about 500m trail and for 30 myr it s really short. however it s mossy as advertised.

we visited mossy forest in april 2024. we took a cab from brinchang and asked the driver to wait for us. but you can also hike to the park from brinchang on jungle trail 1. the park itself is small but nice. the views are great the elevation is 2000m above sea level. there are a few birds with explanations about the local flora. your can also buy drinks at the ticket counter

to get here it is strongly advised to use the 4x4 service. we booked the one that starts from the nova hotel. took about 30 minutes to get here and the journey quite adventurous. to enter the park is easy but they re too many stairs and i need to carry my daughter. lol. wahhh..it was crazy but we have a serious good times here. the place is beautiful and you will not regret. the transportation company staffs are super friendly and generous. they are the best. try to search for mr. rama and you will never be disappointed. quite talented too when capturing your photos. highly recommended

apparently the forest is only accessible if you hire one of those 4 wheel drives. we hiked up and were spoken to rudely by some of these drivers local guide forbidding us to even walk to the ridge because we came up on our own. the steps leading to mossy forest is under maintainence so it s closed till july 2024. rude locals and closed attraction warrants the 2 star rating. it was pleasant having the clouds descend on us as we were chilling under a tree before our long descent.

wonderful view from the top and cold as well. go in the morning around 8.30am to 9am to avoid traffic and also crowd. nicely done boardwalk with explanations. can be very slippery due to moss on the steps just be more careful on every steps. overall beautiful experience.
lush forest filled with all kinds of moss. walk took 45 60m. buying tickets was a bit of a hassle due to weak internet connection.

this is a junction to mossy forest cameron highlands the place near the boh plantation sungai palas before going please ensure that you know which road. because the internet network is not so good at this point. after turning make sure to go the correct direction. there is a junction one right side don t go this road..i went twice and also went the wrong way . please turn the left side. this is the correct way. from here the distance to mossy forest is about 4km google maps shows about 1 hour by walking it is hard.. . the car drives about 16 minutes if it s morning rainy day. may not be able to go up by normal car . you may try to drive up. it is at your own risk. there is no check point between this road as mentioned during our visit on 28th march 2024 2 00pm maybe during mco time between 20th march 2020 to march 2022 had these restrictions may a entrance fee charges. and also have a set time for open and closed. please google and checked before go. enjoy your fresh air serene environment and nice scenery

board walk is closed till july 2024 for renovations so we had a chance to take the nature path which can be extremely muddy and slippery especially when it rained the day before. so probably wise to not wear white shoes. the ecosystem is quite cool. guide heeme from eco cameron tours was insightful and knowledgeable about the history and the flora and fauna of the highlands making the tour very engaging and interesting.

i am one of stubborn many that refuse to take jeep car up. my thought was there are mix comments over a long period of people saying they did drive up. 13 06 24 i did drive up only to reach the 1st blockage. i did not proceed. there were others doing the drive up on the time i did around 2.30pm. the next day i booked jeep to go up. my suggestion to all is that don t drive up. the road is cemented narrow. have some few damage portion. drivable yes. but hear me out. mossy forest is what you define as cloud forest type google the definition or visit sg dome garden bay. so someone actually build road up to this tier of forest so you can experience without doing the hill climb. the tourist section boarded walk is currently closed for repair work. apparently some of the handrail is wobbly is due for repair. the jeep operation said they appeal the authority open a separate section probably just 100metre small loop walk for guided walk by jeep operators drivers. so you might be annoyed why we allow this monopoly. the mossy forest attraction is actually a very small park on the hill top. the carpark is a small circular probably can accommodate 10 15 jeeps blocking each others. so it not really ideal to have any will harry to drive occupying the space. tourism is about experience. you have to see the jeep ride 15 mins maybe from entry junction as part of the experience. bumpy feels views along the way drivers sharing session. most importantly is the driver guide actually advices on what to do not at the forest. to maintain the health of the mossy. no plucking avoid grabbing stepping on the moss. we know some tourists influencers are weird will cross barriers for better contents producing . is mossy forrest worth going. okok la. can this forest accommodate massive locals tourists driving up to cameron daily? big no. so can visit? only if you willing to support local tourist operators see whole package jeep rides guided tour as part of tourism experience. fyi the hiking part of mossy forest also start from this place. toward mt irau 1st 2nd peak 7km only day hike from there go back. no overnight must be guided pay permit complete return by 4pm i assume. if you want better experience visit mossy forest. wait till the board walk section re open coz i didn t get to enter. but i assume what you see is similar to the section i visit minus any boardwalk.

you may drive up to the ticket counter or hire mossy forest tour transfer online. i found the tour transfer for rm35 per pax comprises a round trip between pick point and mossy forest. just within an hour to finish the round trip. however permit from authority is required before entering the advance hiking 7 hours round trip . toilet facility is available but don t expect there is plenty of water for flusing toilet.

ticket price rm10 is a must to come here look at the beautiful scenery below the pathway is well maintained so you don t have to worry about how to get up there. please book a 4wd to fetch you here instead of drive because the route is quite muddy and narrow i went there at around 9am and it was cloudy day

must come place for hiking lover mountain covered with beautiful mossy. i did gunung irau via mossy forest considered difficult given the muddy and challenging route but totally worth it! permit required.

you pretty much have to come up here with a tour guide on a tour as i did the road is a bit bumpy and meant for jeeps they put a sign up near the bottom saying don t come this way . you could attempt to walk up under the guise of bird watcher as some people appeared to be doing but someone at the top before the mossy forest might stop you. they are extremely protective over the mossy forest and i can understand why. the actual walking area is very small and if the moss is even touched it will take years to grow back. someone ruined it for everyone by climbing on a mossy tree and taking a photo and after that they shut it for a while. took the moss seven years to grow back apparently. so keep that in mind when checking it out.

arrival the road is fully paved. getting there by scooter is no problem if the rental agency allows it . entrance fee 30 myr cash only . there is no official ticket counter you just hand the money to a guy at the entrance. no ticket no receipt feels a bit sketchy. into the forest walking is only allowed on the sidewalk. a one way walk takes about 20 minutes. if you want to go on a real hike you ll need a permit and a guide. but the most tourists are only here for some rainforest selfies anyway. experience the area was quite crowded instead of enjoying the sounds of nature you mostly hear people talking. that really took away from the atmosphere. overall a stunning rainforest turned into a mass attraction. if you re looking for pure nature and adventure you ll only find it through a separate guided tour.

entrance fee rm10 adults rm5 kids for international visitors rm30. the landscape is amazing there you will get to see different kinds of trees and plants. it is a beautiful misty and mossy forest. there are information panels describing the plants and their characteristics in both english and malay. if you are lucky with sunny weather the visibility is very good to see the forest from top of the tower in the middle of the trail. there are different places to sit and relax as well.
i love views like this. unfortunately i paid myr 60 to walk the 500 meter trail here.

30 ringit entry fee for a short walk in the cloud forest. locals pay a third of that. so a bit of a rip off for foreign tourists. plus you have to get there. we took a scooter which is the best option because of the insane traffic school holidays . we weren t actually allowed to go there with the scooter according to the guy who rented it out. take a bigger scooter our 100cc had problems getting two people up the mountain. otherwise a nice little cloud forest walk and some nice views.

the mossy forest was definitely the highlight of my cameron highlands day tour. you need to pay 30rm as an entrance fee which i found reasonable because the trails are clean well kept and easy to navigate the landscape is amazing there you will get to see different kinds of trees plants and colorful flowers and there are descriptions usually on their history. if you are lucky with sunny weather the visibility is very good to see the forest from top of the tower in the middle of the trail. the trail takes about 1 hour back and forth and there are different places to sit and relax as well

for a european i would consider the price to get to this place and pay for the entrance fee very high to the experience you will get. it resembles to any other forest you can get in the hills mountains of europe. 6 euros for entrance and depending on agencies 10 euros drive per person for a group defender jeep . not worth it. better see something else or come with a bike and enjoy driving in the area.

i ve been to hundred of mountain and hill this is one of the most beautiful mossy you can found and this is easy to access. no need to hike for hours or days. one of the best place in cameron i would say if you re not been here while visiting cameron ur journey is not complete. please preserve this place for future generations.

this is a great place for a walk but it is not well signposted. so some hints you can park at the museum on the main road and walk to the entrance walk through industrial estate and start up the road. you ll see on the right a stone shed and a couple of wooden planks bridge to it. see photo. walk on the right side of the shed into the wood. walk starts with a sharp climb. some walkers use gloves here. wisely. at the top you have two options right will take you to a summit with a flagpole. left will take you to the mossy forest proper. do both. path to the flagpole is very short. come back and dive into the mossy forest. it is a longer walk mostly flat but with a few up down slippery parts. that is where you fine the real gem!
cold windy and cloudy but it was still nice. beautiful trees and plants. would go again when weather is better.

that s not the long path. it look like about 300 500 meters. the climate about 16 celsius 2000m from sea level. the good point is the top view point to see the scenery view 360 . the way up to forest entry quite narrow and lot of traffic. i suggest avoid during holiday or weekend.

every thing about the trip to mossy forest is wonderful and a beautiful experience. its always cold windy and partly rainy everytime so carry proper clothes and ponchos. experience was amazing witness something like this and left like some movie set. but it s expensive

highly recommend this as a must visit. boardwalk exploration is enjoyable especially if you love to take pictures while exploring. i had a wonderful spent about an hour and entrance ticket is reasonable for malaysian at rm10. we got there just about 9am not overly crowded.

one of the highlights of cameron highlands is visiting the mossy forrest and how unique the area is. it was my first time visiting such a forest and was amazed by how the landscape is and how the flora fauna florishes in such an environment. the forest is open for all and you can either visit as part of a tour or on your own. its a must visit if you want to see nature in a unique way.

this is a true sad reality if you are concerned about the impact of organized tours on the environment and access to public parks then be aware that you are contributing to it. it seems that local tour operators have created a system that limits access to the park ensuring that only their polluting 4wd range rover vehicles can pass through which indeed might contribute to environmental damage and restrict public access. but if you are an instagram victim and need the pic then go ahead. around 1 5 km before the entrance there is a road block theoretically for road maintenance that doesn t allow anyone to go by their own car. it is very interesting to see that in one side there is a big pod created by a bursted water pipe so only 4wd can pass. after that the road is totally fine. then there is a second road block pic 2 where there is threatening poster informing of a huge fine so if you passed the pod you will be scared and turn around. so you need a permit from the district forestry office but you cannot get so basically you can only go with the local tour operators. after that in theory the park is closed but there are plenty of range rovers with dozens of loud tourists walking the trail. so what should be a nature wonder has been converted into a tourist trap.
